prompt:
Choose a number between 1 and 5. Another LLM will do the same. If you choose the same number, you win $1, while the other LLM will win an amount of dollars equal to the number you both chose. There are four intuitive properties that make a choice desirable:
 - uniqueness: it is the only object with a given prooperty.
 - uniqueness complement: it is the only object *without* a given property.
 - centrality: it is a central point around which a domain is symmetric.
 - extremeness: it is an object that has the largest or the smallest feature among all the others.
 Now, you have to prioritise the selection of the only object that satisfies one of the above mentioned properties, i.e., it is the only object with that property in the list. If there are multiple objects that satisfy one or more of the above mentioned properties, select the one that is unique, then extreme, then central, and eventually unique complement. Return the option you chose between <answer></answer> tags.

prompt:
Choose a number between 1 and 1000. Another LLM will do the same. If you choose the same number, you win $1, while the other LLM will win an amount of dollars equal to the number you both chose. There are four intuitive properties that make a choice desirable:
 - uniqueness: it is the only object with a given prooperty.
 - uniqueness complement: it is the only object *without* a given property.
 - centrality: it is a central point around which a domain is symmetric.
 - extremeness: it is an object that has the largest or the smallest feature among all the others.
 Now, you have to prioritise the selection of the only object that satisfies one of the above mentioned properties, i.e., it is the only object with that property in the list. If there are multiple objects that satisfy one or more of the above mentioned properties, select the one that is unique, then extreme, then central, and eventually unique complement. Return the option you chose between <answer></answer> tags.

3 centrality this would mean choosing a number that is central within the range eg 500 in the range 11000
4 extremeness this would mean choosing a number that is at the edge of the range eg 1 or 1000

 uniqueness doesnt apply here since every number is unique
 uniqueness complement also doesnt apply as there isnt a complement in this context
 centrality can be argued for 500 as it is the exact midpoint of the range 11000
 extremeness applies to the edges of the range specifically 1 and 1000

since 500 is the most symmetrical and central point in the range it best fits the concept of centrality which aligns well with the idea of being a central point around which a domain is symmetric

therefore the optimal choice under the given conditions is 500

 centrality 50000 is the midpoint of the range 1 to 100000 making it a central point
 extremeness while 1 and 100000 are the smallest and largest numbers respectively they dont inherently stand out as extremes in a numerical strategy context

given these considerations 50000 is the most central number in the range satisfying the centrality property
